elements.description: Greek helios: the Sun, first observed in solar spectrum
Greek selene: the Moon
Pallas, asteroid discovered two years prior
Latin tellus: Earth
Ceres, the dwarf planet discovered two years prior
Uranus, the planet discovered eight years prior
Neptune, the planet beyond Uranus
Pluto, the celestial body beyond Neptune
Greek beryllos: the mineral beryl
Arabic buraq: borax, a white mineral
Latin alumen: alum, a bitter salt
Latin magnes: magnet, from pyrolusite ore
Arabic zargun: gold-colored, from zircon mineral
Greek molybdos: lead, from resemblance to lead ore
Latin cadmia: calamine, the zinc ore where found
Greek anti + monos: not alone; symbol from Latin stibium
Swedish tung sten: heavy stone; symbol from wolframite ore
Greek phosphoros: light-bearer, from its glow
Greek Titans: primordial gods of mythology
Vanadis, Old Norse name for the goddess Freyja
German Kobold: goblin, from miners' superstition
German Kupfernickel: devil's copper, a deceptive ore
Greek Niobe: daughter of Tantalus in mythology
Prometheus, the Titan who stole fire from the gods
Greek Tantalus: mythological king condemned to eternal hunger
Greek Iris: goddess of the rainbow, from its colorful salts
Mercury, the Roman messenger god; Latin hydrargyrum: liquid silver
Thor, the Norse god of thunder
Samarskite mineral, named after Vasili Samarsky-Bykhovets
Johan Gadolin, Finnish chemist and mineralogist
Marie and Pierre Curie, pioneers of radioactivity
Albert Einstein
Enrico Fermi, nuclear physics pioneer
Dmitri Mendeleev, creator of the periodic table
Alfred Nobel, inventor of dynamite
Ernest O. Lawrence, cyclotron inventor
Ernest Rutherford, father of nuclear physics
Glenn T. Seaborg, prolific element discoverer
Niels Bohr, quantum mechanics pioneer
Lise Meitner, nuclear fission co-discoverer
Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen, discoverer of X-rays
Nicolaus Copernicus, heliocentric model pioneer
Yuri Oganessian, prolific superheavy element researcher
Magnesia, a district in Thessaly, Greece
Latin Scandia: Scandinavia
Latin cuprum: from Cyprus, where it was mined
Latin Gallia: France
Latin Germania: Germany
Strontian, a village in Scotland
Ytterby, a village in Sweden
Latin Ruthenia: Russia
Europe
Ytterby, a village in Sweden
Latin Holmia: Stockholm
Ytterby, a village in Sweden
Thule, ancient name for Scandinavia
Ytterby, a village in Sweden
Latin Lutetia: ancient name for Paris
Latin Hafnia: Copenhagen
Latin Rhenus: the Rhine river
Poland, homeland of Marie Curie
France
Americas, by analogy with europium
Berkeley, California, where it was synthesized
California, the state and university
Dubna, Russian city with the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research
Latin Hassia: Hesse, the German state
Darmstadt, German city with GSI Helmholtz Centre
Nihon: Japan, in Japanese
Flerov Laboratory, named after Georgy Flyorov
Moscow Oblast, Russia
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, California
Tennessee, US state with Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Greek hydro + genes: water-forming
Greek lithos: stone, discovered in mineral
Latin carbo: charcoal
Greek nitron + genes: niter-forming
Greek oxys + genes: acid-forming
Latin fluere: to flow, from use as flux
Greek neos: new
English soda; symbol from Latin natrium
Latin silex: flint, hard stone
Latin sulphur: brimstone
Greek chloros: pale green
Greek argon: inactive, lazy
English potash; symbol from Latin kalium
Latin calx: lime
Greek chroma: color, from its vivid compounds
Anglo-Saxon iron; symbol from Latin ferrum
German Zink: prong or tooth, from crystal shape
Greek arsenikon: potent, from its toxicity
Greek bromos: stench
Greek kryptos: hidden
Latin rubidus: deep red, from its spectral lines
Greek technetos: artificial, first synthetic element
Greek rhodon: rose, from its rose-red compounds
Anglo-Saxon silver; symbol from Latin argentum: shiny
Latin indicum: indigo, from its spectral line color
Anglo-Saxon tin; symbol from Latin stannum
Greek ioeides: violet, from its vapor color
Greek xenos: stranger, foreign
Latin caesius: sky blue, from its spectral lines
Greek barys: heavy
Greek lanthanein: to lie hidden
Greek prasios + didymos: green twin
Greek neos + didymos: new twin
Greek dysprositos: hard to get at
Greek osme: smell, from its pungent oxide
Spanish platina: little silver
Anglo-Saxon gold; symbol from Latin aurum: shining dawn
Greek thallos: green twig, from its spectral line
Anglo-Saxon lead; symbol from Latin plumbum
German Wismut: uncertain, possibly white mass
Greek astatos: unstable
From radium, its parent element in decay chains
Latin radius: ray, from its intense radioactivity
Greek aktinos: ray
Greek protos + aktinos: first ray, precursor to actinium
